text: Options for Queue Based Enrichment System
My thoughts on the requirements to discuss:
 Expected number of enrichments per minute/hour
o Likely up to 100 per minute, like if we’re running 4 enrichments across 15 docs   Ability to process enrichments simultaneously
o I think this is necessary because it lets the system run at full capacity w/o
worrying about enrichments   One enrichment type per enrichment request
o If multiple per request, is sequential processing necessary?  o I think we’re only dealing with one per request right now, but that could change in
the future.   Criticality of retry mechanisms, low
o We just need to store that a job failed as we discussed Friday.   Audit and job history trails are necessary
o Make sure we know what happens with every job   Persistence is not necessary across service restarts
o We just need to know that the last job of the prior service’s life failed   Job processing delays are not too big of a deal
o This is a backend service behind a backend service, users can wait to access the
embeddings of their docs   Are enrichments CPU bound? I/O bound?

o Embeddings: I/O bound, has to wait for OpenAI  o Date extraction: CPU-bound due to text parsing, pattern recognition  o Glossary: mostly I/O due to Neo operations  o Summary: I/O bound, uses OpenAI   What are the memory constraints of the system?   OpenAI gives external API rate limits
o This slows us down some   Job priority is a necessity
o Do enrichments first because they are more important   Is job scheduling necessary? I think not.
o Jobs can be run ASAP, esp if push based queue system   Do we need to be able to cancel jobs? I think not.
o Have never spoken about this, seems unnecessary   Real-time status monitoring is necessary
o Need to have real time status of jobs   We need metrics for things like queue depth, processing times, and failure rates
o More data is more good
Option 1: Current System
Pros  Cons  Simple, currently functional  No persistence  Familiar codebase  Partial job history  No new dependencies  Limited monitoring  Low memory footprint  Single threaded    No rate limiting
Option 2: Piscena as Queue and Worker Threads

Pros  Cons  True parallelism  No persistence  Simple architecture  No native job history or audit trail  Good for CPU bound work  No built-in monitoring   Low latency  No retry mechanism    No priority handling    No rate limiting
Option 3: BullMQ without Worker Threads
Pros  Cons  Full persistence  Single threaded  Excellent job history, monitoring, and  auditing
Redis dependency
Built in rate limiting  Slower for CPU tasks  Native support for priority queues
Option 4: BullMQ + Piscena Worker Threads
Pros  Cons  All BullMQ pros  More complex  Parallelism  Higher memory usage  Best performance  More dependencies  Flexible resource allocation  Debugging complexity
